mnemonics.title: Instruction Cycle Phases
mnemonics.content: F-D-E-S: First Drink Early Soda (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Store)

mnemonics.title: CPU Registers Remembering
mnemonics.content: PC: Points to Next, IR: Instruction Ready, MAR: Memory Address Required, MBR: Memory Buffer Ready

acronyms.usage: Holds address of next instruction to fetch
Holds memory address for read/write operations
Alternative name for MBR, stores data being transferred
Generates control signals for instruction execution
Specifies the operation to be performed

visualTips.visualization: Think of instruction as a train: Engine (opcode) pulls the cars (operands). Zero-address = engine only, One-address = engine + 1 car, Two-address = engine + 2 cars, Three-address = engine + 3 cars.
Fetch = delivery truck bringing work, Decode = supervisor reading work order, Execute = workers doing the job, Store = shipping finished product.

visualTips.visualize: PC = reception desk (next task), IR = manager's desk (current task), MAR = address book, MBR = clipboard, Accumulator = calculator memory.

analogies.analogy: Like recipes: Zero-address is 'stir' (uses what's in bowl), One-address is 'add sugar' (sugar specified), Two-address is 'mix flour and water', Three-address is 'add result of X and Y to Z'.
Like reading a to-do list: Fetch = pick up list, Decode = understand task, Execute = do task, Store = write down result. Repeat for next task.
Finding a friend: Direct = you know their house address, Indirect = you know someone who knows their address, Immediate = friend is standing right there, Register = friend's number is in your phone.
Opcode is the verb (action), operand is the noun (object). Like 'eat cake' - eat is opcode, cake is operand.
Just as a conductor coordinates different instruments to play in harmony, the CU coordinates CPU components to execute instructions in sequence.
